deep_analysis.executive_intro:
This FTSE screen is broadly cautious: 147 holds and 40 avoids outweigh 16 strong buys and 45 buys, so outright conviction is concentrated in a small subset rather than widespread. All five top candidates are strong buys with full 20/20 data quality and five-factor passes (cheapness, quality, dividend, GARP, risk), but conviction scores sit in a narrow 51–54% band and every name is a new signal (one week at signal), so persistence is unproven. Sector clustering is notable: three of five top picks are Industrials (FGP.L, MEGP.L, GFTU.L), with Healthcare (HIK.L) and Communication Services (ITV.L) rounding out the list. One caution: several model failure reasons reference missing NCAV/balance-sheet detail, negative or weak earnings growth, and free-cash conversion gaps—the screen is strong on trailing multiples and composite ranks but cannot fully validate balance-sheet depth, cyclical trough timing, or qualitative moats.

FGP.L (FirstGroup plc) — Industrials. Eleven of 22 models passed (composite 90%, sector-relative 84%), led by Earnings Yield (E/P 11.0%, P/E 9.1), FCF Yield (31.2%), Low P/E + High Yield, Neff PEGY, Magic Formula, Acquirer's Multiple, Piotroski F-Score (8/9), Composite Value, and Earnings Quality; key metrics include P/B 1.4, yield 4.0%, and ROE 16.7%. Failures on Graham Enterprising (excessive leverage, negative earnings growth −5.9%), Financial Health (high debt to equity, weak liquidity), and Economic Moat (thin margins, high leverage) temper the quality read. Adds to Industrials concentration alongside MEGP.L and GFTU.L. Verdict: watchlist — exceptional headline value and cash metrics, but leverage, liquidity, and contracting earnings growth need confirmation before sizing up.

MEGP.L (ME Group International plc) — Industrials. Fourteen of 22 models passed (composite 81%, sector-relative 88%), including Graham Enterprising, Earnings Yield (E/P 14.1%, P/E 7.1), Low P/E + High Yield, Quality Value, Buffett Quality, Economic Moat, High Dividend Yield (8.0%, universe rank 97%), Magic Formula, and Acquirer's Multiple; ROE 28.2% and D/E 28% stand out. Model failures flag negative earnings growth (−3.9%), FCF yield 3.9% (below the 5% hurdle), weak free-cash conversion on Earnings Quality, and Piotroski F-Score 6/9. Third Industrials name in the top five increases sector concentration risk. Verdict: watchlist — cheapest and highest-yield profile with moat-model support, but shrinking earnings and cash conversion warrant proof that dividend is sustainable.

HIK.L (Hikma Pharmaceuticals PLC) — Healthcare. Thirteen of 22 models passed (composite 75%, sector-relative 91%), including Graham Enterprising, Earnings Yield (P/E 11.7), Lynch PEG (PEG 0.50, growth 23.5%), Neff PEGY, Quality Value, Buffett Quality, Dividend Growth, Magic Formula, Acquirer's Multiple, and Financial Health (D/E 62%). Key metrics: P/B 1.8, yield 4.0%, ROE 16.5%. Critical failures: FCF Yield (−2.0%; reported FCF −66.1m), Earnings Quality (weak free-cash conversion), and Piotroski F-Score 6/9. Diversifies away from the Industrials cluster. Verdict: watchlist — attractive earnings growth and valuation versus healthcare peers, but negative free cash flow is a material gap the quant stack cannot explain.

ITV.L (ITV plc) — Communication Services. Fourteen of 22 models passed (composite 84%, sector-relative 80%), including Graham Enterprising, Earnings Yield, FCF Yield (9.1%), Lynch PEG (PEG 0.47, growth 25.6%), Neff PEGY, Quality Value, High Dividend Yield (6.8%, universe rank 95%), Dividend Growth, Composite Value, Earnings Quality, and Financial Health (D/E 48%, interest coverage 15.1x). Only top name with favourable entry timing (RSI 35, accumulate signal). Piotroski F-Score 3/9 is a stark internal contradiction. Verdict: accumulate — best risk/reward entry among top picks on timing and income/value combo, provided structural media risks are acceptable.

GFTU.L (Grafton Group plc) — Industrials. Eleven of 22 models passed (composite 84%, sector-relative 80%), including Graham Defensive, Graham Enterprising, Schloss Low P/B (P/B 1.1, D/E 38%), FCF Yield (10.0%), Neff PEGY, Piotroski F-Score (8/9), Composite Value, Earnings Quality, and Financial Health (current ratio 2.11, coverage 6.8x). P/E 12.6 and yield 4.0% with positive earnings growth 8.4%, but ROE 8.4% failed Quality Value, Buffett Quality, and Economic Moat. RSI 63 and price above the 200-day MA suggest less margin of safety on timing. Verdict: accumulate — strongest balance-sheet and Graham-style profile in the Industrials trio, acceptable for phased entry despite cyclical exposure and modest returns on equity.

- FGP.L: UK public-transport operator — franchise renewal and regulatory pricing risk not in the screen; transport sector pension deficits are a common off-balance-sheet concern (open question: defined-benefit funding status); model failures already flag excessive leverage, weak liquidity, and negative earnings growth.
- MEGP.L: Airport/travel-adjacent photobooth and vending exposure implies cyclicality tied to passenger volumes; negative earnings growth (−3.9%) alongside an 8.0% yield raises dividend sustainability and governance/payout-policy questions; small-cap liquidity and disclosure depth are unverified.
- HIK.L: Generic injectables/pharma faces FDA regulatory risk, patent cliffs, and pricing pressure in US markets; negative FCF (−66.1m) may reflect growth capex or working-capital build — the screen cannot distinguish; pipeline quality and litigation exposure are open questions.
- ITV.L: Structural shift from linear broadcast to streaming threatens long-run advertising share; ad revenue is highly cyclical; Piotroski F-Score 3/9 suggests deteriorating operational momentum despite strong headline value — balance-sheet health alone may mask earnings-quality drift.
- GFTU.L: Building-materials distribution is tied to UK/Ireland housing and construction cycles; ROE 8.4% and thin margins (failed moat models) imply limited pricing power in a downturn; geographic and M&A integration risks from prior acquisitions are not captured.
deep_analysis.red_flags:
NAMES WORTH DEEPER RESEARCH

question_outcomes.question: What drives negative free cash flow (−$66.1m) and is it temporary capex or a structural cash-conversion problem?
question_outcomes.evidence: Trailing FCF −$66.1m (`screening_snapshot.json`) diverges from FY2025 FCF +$119m (Yahoo `financials_annual.json`; reconciled in FY2025 results body `filings/bodies/ir_0e40d9707e30c3b7.txt`). Management attributes OCF compression to **$186m one-off legal settlements** (chiefly Xyrem; $111m restricted cash at 31 Dec 2025, paid January 2026); excluding these, OCF would have risen ~10%. FY2025 drivers: working-capital outflow −$251m (inventory +$86m, receivables +$97m), CapEx **$317m** (PPE $197m + intangibles $120m), dividends $185m. 2026 CapEx guidance **$190–210m**; Bedford commercial production not until **2028**. H1 2025 OCF was only **$161m** (`ir_a70365d580129295.txt`), explaining trailing negativity versus full-year figures.

documents.name: ITV plc
documents.executive_summary: ITV plc screens as a **Strong Buy** on cheapness, dividend yield, quality and risk metrics, with the share price trading below its 200-day moving average and RSI near 35. The central investment case is a discounted UK media asset undergoing structural simplification: the agreed **£1.6bn sale of the Media & Entertainment (M&E) broadcast and streaming arm to Sky**, with management citing a **£950m shareholder return**, would leave ITV Studios as a standalone global content producer. Valuation looks compelling on headline multiples (P/E ~12, yield ~6.8%, P/B ~1.5), but the key debate is whether post-deal ITV Studios can sustain earnings, cash flow and the dividend without the broadcast cash engine — and whether CMA/regulatory approval is secured on acceptable terms. **Primary filing body text is unavailable in the source pack**, so financial conclusions rely on Yahoo fallback data and must be verified before trading.
documents.investment_thesis:
The quantitative screen rates ITV a **Strong Buy** (14/22 models passed, composite score 84%, sector-relative 80%), spanning cheapness, quality, dividend, GARP and risk families with full metric coverage (20/20). Headline metrics — **P/E 12.2, P/B 1.5, dividend yield 6.8%, ROE 12.3%, debt/equity 48%** — fit a classic value profile: a cash-generative business trading below book-adjusted fair value with an income kicker. Passed models include Graham Enterprising, Earnings Yield, FCF Yield, Lynch PEG, Neff PEGY, Magic Formula, Acquirer's Multiple, High Dividend Yield, Dividend Growth, Earnings Quality and Financial Health, suggesting the screen sees both valuation support and balance-sheet resilience.

Business quality, as inferred from Yahoo financials (fallback), shows a group that has stabilised revenue after a multi-year decline and restored operating profit from the 2023 trough, albeit with volatile reported net income driven by one-offs. **ITV Studios** — the intended remaining entity — is a scaled UK/international production business with franchise IP (e.g. Love Island, broad drama slate) and third-party commissions; the screen's quality and earnings-quality passes imply acceptable accounting and return characteristics on a normalised basis. The **Sky M&E disposal** is the value-unlock catalyst: monetising a structurally challenged linear-TV asset at ~£1.6bn, reducing capital intensity and funding returns, while refocusing equity on higher-growth, asset-light content production.

Timing overlay from the screen is **Accumulate** (timing score 0.75): RSI 35 and a ~8% discount to the 200-day MA suggest favourable entry conditions for a value accumulator, though conviction is only 51% given the signal is new (one week). For a value investor, the asymmetry is clear: downside is partly cushioned by yield and tangible deal proceeds; upside hinges on pro-forma Studios earnings power and capital return execution.

FINANIAL REVIEW

**Source limitation:** The filings index catalogues six UK RNS/Companies House entries (four annual, one interim, one other) but **none have downloadable body extracts** (`filings/bodies/` is empty; `with_body: 0`). All figures below are from **Yahoo `financials_annual.json` (fallback)** and should not be treated as filing-verified. Filing headlines indexed but not parsed: *Full Year Results 2024* (6 Mar 2025), *Full Year Results 2025* (5 Mar 2026), *Q1 Trading Update* (14 May 2026), Companies House group accounts (May 2025, May 2026), and *Sale of ITV M&E Business to Sky* (6 Jul 2026). **No half-year/interim results release body is present** beyond the Q1 trading update headline; interim financial detail is a gap.

**Income statement trend (Yahoo, £m)**

| | 2022 | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Revenue | 3,728 | 3,624 | 3,488 | 3,511 |
| Operating income | 519 | 238 | 318 | 363 |
| EBITDA | 656 | 344 | 734 (norm. 522) | 473 |
| Net income | 428 | 210 | 408 | 220 |

Revenue fell **~5%** from 2022 to 2024 before a modest **+0.7%** rebound in 2025. Operating income collapsed in 2023 (likely reflecting advertising weakness and cost pressures) and partially recovered; 2025 operating income of **£363m** is the strongest "clean" year in the period. **2024 net income of £408m is not comparable**: Yahoo flags **£212m unusual items** (consistent with disposal/gain activity; cash flow shows **£295m business sale proceeds**). Normalised 2024 earnings would be materially lower. **2025 net income of £220m** represents a more representative run-rate, down ~46% from reported 2024 but up on the 2023 trough.

**Cash flow and capital allocation (Yahoo, £m)**

| | 2022 | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Operating cash flow | 304 | 385 | 333 | 202 |
| CapEx | (78) | (70) | (49) | (54) |
| Free cash flow | 226 | 315 | 284 | **148** |
| Dividends paid | (201) | (201) | (198) | (187) |

FCF generation was robust in 2023–24 but **weakened sharply in 2025 to £148m**, driven by a **£196m working-capital outflow** (receivables build). Dividend cash outflow has been steady at **~£187–201m p.a.**, implying the current yield is supported historically but **2025 FCF covered dividends only ~79%** — a yellow flag for sustainability absent deal proceeds or a rebased payout post-disposal. The screen's FCF Yield pass may reflect a trailing or normalised view; verify against FY2025 RNS.

**Balance sheet (Yahoo, year-end £m)**

| | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---:|---:|
| Total assets | 4,186 | 4,285 |
| Stockholders' equity | 1,808 | 1,798 |
| Total debt | 838 | 876 |
| Net debt | 306 | **463** |
| Cash | 427 | 302 |
| Goodwill + intangibles | 1,533 | 1,531 |
| Tangible book value | 275 | **267** |

Net debt rose **£157m** in 2025 to **£463m** as cash fell; leverage remains moderate (D/E ~48% per screen) but trending unfavourably. **Goodwill and intangibles of ~£1.5bn** dominate the balance sheet; tangible equity is thin at **£267m**, so P/B metrics are sensitive to impairment assumptions — relevant if M&E separation triggers accounting reassessment. Defined benefit pension obligations are **£231m** (2025); non-current pension liabilities **£43m**. No filing-body language on going concern, covenant headroom or contingencies is available to assess.

**Interim / trading update:** A *Q1 Trading Update* (14 May 2026) is indexed but without body text; no quantitative interim figures can be cited. News flow around that date referenced World Cup advertising optimism and Sky deal hopes, but that is not substitutable for verified interim accounts.

**Annual results narrative (from news, not filings):** FY2025 results (5 Mar 2026) were reported in secondary sources as beating expectations despite profit decline, with digital revenue growth and cost management cited; total advertising revenue was described as weaker. FY2024 results (6 Mar 2025) coincided with ongoing Sky talks. These headlines align with the Yahoo revenue stabilisation and earnings normalisation pattern but cannot replace primary filing review.
documents.financial_review:
**Source limitation (gap-fill pass):** `gap_fill_source_map.json` confirms nine filings indexed (seven annual, one interim, one other) with **zero body extracts** after five refetch attempts. All figures remain from **Yahoo `financials_annual.json` (fallback)**. Indexed but unparseable: FY2024/FY2025 results, Q1 Trading Update (14 May 2026), five Companies House group accounts (2022–2026), and *SALE OF ITV M&E BUSINESS TO SKY* (6 Jul 2026). No half-year results body.

**Income statement (Yahoo fallback, £m):** Revenue 3,728 → 3,511 (2022–2025), still ~6% below 2022; operating income recovered to £363m in 2025; 2024 net income £408m distorted by £212m unusual items; 2025 net income £220m is the cleaner run-rate.

**Cash flow and dividend (Yahoo fallback, £m):** 2025 FCF £148m vs dividends £187m (~79% coverage), following a £196m working-capital outflow. Screen FCF Yield pass uses ~£249m FCF (`screening_snapshot.json`) — **unreconciled** with Yahoo 2025.

**Balance sheet (Yahoo fallback):** Net debt rose to £463m (from £306m); tangible book £267m vs equity £1.8bn; pension obligation £231m (up from £207m). Alternate news notes Box Clever transfer into ITV Pension Scheme (Pensions Expert, 7 Nov 2025); funding detail missing.

**Segment/interim colour (news fallback):** Studios margins at low end of range despite revenue growth (Proactive Investors, 14 May 2026); Q3 revenue +2% with strong digital (DirectorsTalk, 6 Nov 2025); World Cup ad optimism (Proactive Investors, 14 May 2026).

**Corporate action (news/index only):** Sky M&E acquisition ~£1.6bn (FT, 6 Jul 2026); £950m shareholder return cited (9 Jul 2026). Pro-forma Studios accounts not in local sources.

**Remaining gaps:** segment tables, post-deal dividend policy, covenants, going-concern language, screen-vs-Yahoo FCF reconciliation.

documents.risks_and_flags:
**Evidenced — structural/cyclical:** Revenue below 2022 peak; weaker total advertising per FY2025 news; post-Sky Studios loses broadcast integration benefits.

**Evidenced — earnings quality:** Piotroski absent from passed_models; email brief cites 3/9; Yahoo 2025 OCF < net income and ROA decline vs 2024. Financial Health passes on leverage alone.

**Evidenced — dividend/FCF:** 79% FCF coverage in 2025; yield may be supported by £950m planned return rather than recurring Studios cash flow.

**Evidenced — deal/regulatory:** £1.6bn Sky sale; Guardian (Pratley, Nov 2025) flagged price, politics, regulation.

**Evidenced — intangibles/pension:** Tangible book £267m; goodwill ~£1.5bn; pension £231m with incomplete funding disclosure.

**Open — disclosure:** No filing bodies after refetch → no going-concern, covenant, or contingency language. Close via Companies House PDF, Investegate RNS re-pull, IR presentation.

**Open — screen caveats:** Conviction 51.3%, one week at signal; universe broadly cautious per email brief.

documents.name: Hikma Pharmaceuticals PLC
documents.executive_summary:
Hikma Pharmaceuticals is a London-listed, US-dollar-reporting generics and injectables group trading on a value screen of P/E 11.2, P/B 1.8, dividend yield 4.0%, and ROE 16.5% — metrics that place it in the top decile of UK healthcare on the quantitative model. The investment case rests on a diversified three-division franchise (Injectables, Generics, Branded), earnings recovery from 2023 impairment trough, and capital return via dividends and a buyback launched in February 2026. The central debate is whether February 2026’s guidance reset and working-capital-heavy cash flow are transient execution noise or evidence of structural margin and US pricing pressure. Primary filing bodies were not available in the research corpus, limiting verification of management guidance, contingencies, and covenant language.

---
documents.investment_thesis:
The quantitative screen flags Hikma as a **Strong Buy**, passing 13 of 22 models across five families: cheapness, quality, dividend, GARP, and risk. Standout passes include Graham Enterprising, Lynch PEG, Neff PEGY, Buffett Quality, Magic Formula, Acquirer’s Multiple, and Financial Health — a profile consistent with a profitable, cash-generative compounder trading below intrinsic multiples rather than a distressed cyclical.

**Valuation.** At P/E 11.2 and earnings yield ~9%, the shares embed little growth despite revenue compounding at roughly 7% annually over 2022–2025 (Yahoo fallback). P/B 1.8 on ROE 16.5% suggests the market prices Hikma closer to a mature generic manufacturer than a quality compounder with injectables exposure.

**Business quality.** Hikma operates across injectables (hospital channel, higher barriers), US/EU/MENA generics, and a smaller branded portfolio — a structure that diversifies patent-cliff and pricing risk relative to single-product peers. Normalised earnings have recovered sharply: reported net income rose from $190m (2023) to $359m (2024) and $402m (2025), with diluted EPS of $1.81 (2025) versus $0.85 (2023).

**Shareholder returns.** Dividends paid rose from $137m (2023) to $185m (2025). News flow confirms an active buyback programme from February 2026 and ongoing treasury purchases through July 2026. Chairman Victoria Hull increased her holding materially in May 2025 — a modest governance positive, though not dispositive.

**Why value investors should care.** The screen combines cheap absolute multiples with quality and dividend support — rare in healthcare. If 2026 guidance (reiterated in April 2026 per news) holds and free cash flow normalises after 2025 working-capital absorption, the risk/reward skews favourable without requiring heroic growth assumptions.

---
documents.financial_review:
**Source hierarchy.** Primary: FY2025 combined results (`filings/bodies/ir_0e40d9707e30c3b7.txt` — includes audited cash-flow statement and going-concern note). Secondary: H1 2025 presentation (`ir_a70365d580129295.txt`), annual report front/strategic sections (`ir_3a67962eb8770824.txt`). Tertiary: Yahoo `financials_annual.json`. Companies House accounts (five annual filings) remain **indexed only** (`has_body: false`).

**Earnings.** FY2025 reported revenue **$3,349m** (+7%); profit attributable to shareholders **$402m** (+12%); core operating profit **$741m** (+3%) at margin **22.1%**. Reported operating profit **$542m** (−11%) reflects Xyrem legal settlement and other exceptional items. Injectables core margin fell to **31.0%** (from 35.3%); management guides **27–28%** for 2026.

**Cash flow — the central gap.** FY2025 operating cash flow **$436m** (−23% YoY); filing attributes decline primarily to **$186m settlement cash**; ex-settlement OCF would have grown ~10%. Free cash flow **$119m** (OCF minus $317m CapEx). Trailing screen FCF **−$66.1m** reflects recent-quarter consumption (H1 2025 OCF **$161m** vs $198m prior year; settlement paid post year-end; concurrent **$250m buyback** launch and **$185m dividends**). Working-capital days **245** (2024: 240). This is **partly temporary** (one-off legal cash, settlement timing) and **partly structural** (elevated CapEx $190–210m guided for 2026; Bedford revenues from 2028; ongoing WC build in a wholesaler-chargeback model).

**Balance sheet.** Net debt **$1,387m**; leverage **1.6×** net debt/core EBITDA (2024: 1.4×). Upgraded to **BBB** (S&P and Fitch). Going-concern affirmed; filing states covenants on major debt are **suspended while investment-grade status is maintained** — specific covenant thresholds not in extracted text.

**Capital returns.** Total dividend **84c** (+5%); final **48c** recommended. Buyback up to **$250m** in 2026. OCF covers dividends ~2.4×; FCF does not. Dividend safety through the next generic cycle remains **open**.

**Guidance.** 2026 Group revenue **+2% to +4%**; core operating profit **$720–770m**. Medium-term Group and Injectables margin guidance **withdrawn** (Feb 2026 results). April 2026 reiteration reported in news only — **PDF body not ingested**.

**Remaining gaps.** Segment-level FCF; quarterly cash bridge for trailing −$66.1m; pension/post-employment detail (only −$2m remeasurement in filing); product-level patent cliff schedule; April 2026 trading-update full text.

---
documents.risks_and_flags:
**Evidenced (filings + Yahoo + news headlines).**
- **Cash conversion:** Trailing FCF negative; FY2025 FCF $119m below dividends + buyback commitment; WC days rising.
- **US Injectables pressure:** Competition on testosterone, calcitonin and other high-value products; margin guide down 400bp+; mix shift to lower-margin partnered/Xellia products.
- **Litigation:** Xyrem settlement ~$72m cash (disclosed in FY2025 results Note 5); sodium oxybate competition flagged for Hikma Rx in 2026 outlook.
- **Leverage:** Net debt +$269m YoY (filing); refinancing activity ($500m Eurobond, IFC and syndicated loans).
- **Governance/strategy volatility:** CEO return, board reshuffle, medium-term guidance withdrawal (Feb 2026).
- **503B exposure:** Compounding business in “Others” at FY2025; news reports exit in April 2026 — **not confirmed in filing bodies**.

**Open — source to close.**
| Risk | Status | Next source |
|------|--------|-------------|
| FDA enforcement / quality warning letters | Open | FDA OAI/WL database; annual report risk section pp. 80–88 |
| Patent cliff timing (e.g. tafamidis/VYNDAMAX) | Headline only | Settlement filing text; IR pipeline deck |
| Dividend through-cycle policy | Open | FY2025 dividend policy section (full annual report); April 2026 update PDF |
| Pension deficit / cash contributions | Open | Companies House 2025 accounts PDF |
| Covenant headroom (quantified) | Partial | Debt note in statutory accounts; credit agreement summaries |
| 503B exit financial impact | Open | April 2026 trading-update PDF |

**Screen blind spot.** Strong Buy signal persists despite negative trailing FCF, failed FCF Yield/Earnings Quality screens, and concurrent dividend plus buyback — cash-return overlay not applied.

---

- Multiple Yahoo Finance pieces (Mar–May 2026) document analyst target resets to £16–£20 cluster after the guidance event; JPMorgan cited at Overweight / £20.

**Sector context**

documents.research_rationale: Gap-fill **partially resolves** the FCF puzzle (settlement cash, CapEx cycle, WC timing) and confirms cheap earnings-based valuation, but **weakens** the Strong Buy case by validating persistent cash-conversion risk, concurrent capital returns above FCF, and withdrawn medium-term guidance.

documents.name: ME Group International plc
documents.executive_summary:
ME Group International operates a global network of photobooths and unattended laundry machines, generating recurring, asset-heavy cash flows from high-footfall locations. The quantitative screen flags a **strong buy** on cheapness (P/E 7.6, yield 7.3%), quality (ROE 28.2%), and balance-sheet strength (debt/equity ~28%), yet the shares sit below their 200-day moving average after a sharp June 2026 sell-off tied to a profit-guidance cut. The central debate is whether trailing financial strength and laundry expansion offset cyclical photobooth softness, governance scrutiny, and a dividend that now exceeds reported free cash flow. Primary RNS filing bodies were not available in the source pack, limiting verification of management’s revised FY2026 outlook.

---
documents.investment_thesis:
The screen’s **strong buy** (14/22 models passed; composite score 78%; sector-relative 87%) rests on a rare combination for a UK small/mid-cap industrial: deep value multiples, high returns on equity, a covered-looking earnings yield, and passing scores across cheapness, quality, dividend, GARP, and risk families. Yahoo-sourced financials (fallback — see Financial Review) show five years of revenue and profit growth, material deleveraging, and operating cash conversion well above net income.

For a value investor, the hook is a profitable, installed-base business trading at roughly **7–8× earnings** with a **7%+ dividend yield**, where capital is redeployed into laundry roll-out and booth refresh rather than speculative growth. The laundry segment — including the April 2026 Asda collaboration — offers a second leg that is less discretionary than photobooth usage and is currently offsetting weaker booth trading per recent news flow. Peel Hunt reiterated a buy rating on 13 July 2026, aligning with the quantitative signal despite near-term guidance uncertainty.

The screen’s **new** signal (one week at strong buy; conviction 49%) and **neutral timing** (RSI ~57, price below 200-day MA) suggest fundamental appeal without a confirmed price inflection — appropriate for phased accumulation rather than aggressive chasing.

---
documents.financial_review:
**Source hierarchy.** Primary: H1 FY2026 interim RNS (`filings/bodies/ir_3f2f55026f8c6fe6.txt`), FY2025 results (`ir_a006e8bd9b660a9c.txt`), annual reports (`ir_55af4b8f27e3dd6f.txt`, `ir_9ac08315f9388f7e.txt`). Secondary: Yahoo `financials_annual.json`. Companies House accounts (five annual filings) remain **indexed only** (`has_body: false`).

H1 FY2026 (to 30 April): revenue **£154.3m (+0.3%)**, EBITDA **£57.0m (+7.1%)**, PBT **£32.7m (−3.8%)**, diluted EPS **6.48p (−3.9%)**, cash from operations **£38.7m (−18.9%)**, capex **£33.9m**, net cash **£7.5m** (ex leases). Wash.ME vending **+16.3%**; Photo.ME **−6.2%**. Interim dividend **3.60p (−6.5%)**; policy **>55% of PAT** reaffirmed. FY PBT guidance **£69–74m**; going-concern affirmed.

FY2025 (Yahoo + results body): NI **£56.6m (+4.6%)**, OCF **£90.8m**, FCF **£25.2m**, dividends paid **£29.8m** (FCF cover **0.84×**; OCF cover **~3.0×**). Total dividend **8.64p** (+9.5%). Net cash fell from **£26.5m to £7.5m** since October 2025 as **£34.0m** returned via dividends and buybacks.

Screen (`screening_snapshot.json`): **P/E 7.1**, **yield 8.0%**, **FCF yield 3.9%**, **Piotroski 6/9**, **21% below 200-day MA**.

**Remaining gaps:** June 2026 trading-update RNS body; Companies House PDF text (covenant, pension, NCAV); segment maintenance vs growth capex split.

documents.risks_and_flags:
**Evidenced:** April France photobooth slowdown and Photo.ME −6.2% (H1 body); H1 EPS/PBT decline matching screen −3.9%; FCF yield 3.9% vs dividend yield 8.0%; FY2025 FCF < dividends; H1 cash from ops −18.9%; interim dividend cut 6.5%; net cash compression to £7.5m; going-concern OK.

**Partially mitigated:** >55% PAT dividend policy reaffirmed; gross cash £52.8m; May/June trading recovery cited.

**Still open:** governance severity (Investors' Chronicle unverified in filings); covenant/pension detail (CH PDFs missing); June guidance-cut bridge; small-cap liquidity; final dividend at revised FY PBT.

### 2026-07-20T20:48:07.945587+00:00
Q: Consumer defensive label may mask commodity or agricultural cyclicality (plantations); governance, land/asset revaluation policy, and pension/off-balance-sheet liabilities are not captured — insufficient data in JSON to assess.

Status: partially_resolved

Evidence: Yahoo `financials_annual.json` shows clear palm-oil cyclicality (revenue $447.6m FY2022 → $370.4m FY2023 → $372.3m FY2024 → $465.2m FY2025), and news/alternate news tie share weakness to Indonesian export policy (“Palm oil stocks struggle after Indonesian intervention”, Investors’ Chronicle, 27 May 2026; RNS headline “Indonesian Government Export Proposals”, 20 May 2026). Yahoo records a modest on-balance-sheet pension obligation ($8.0m non-current, FY2025) but no land revaluation policy, governance structure, or off-balance-sheet/contingent liability disclosure — `filings/bodies/` is empty (0 files) and no annual report body is indexed.

SourcesTried: filings_bodies, filings_index, yahoo_financials, news_manifest, alternate_news, screening_snapshot

NextSources: Companies House filed accounts / annual report PDF (pensions, contingencies, going-concern, related-party and governance notes); Company IR / results presentation PDF (segment margins, estate valuation basis, Indonesia structure post-Pinago)

---

Q: What drives zero reported D/E and a current ratio of 8.69: net-cash balance sheet, accounting classification, or data anomaly?

Status: resolved

Evidence: Yahoo FY2025 balance sheet reconciles the screen metrics without inconsistency: total debt $0.5m (entirely capital-lease obligations; no material bank borrowings), equity $580.7m, cash $231.8m plus short-term investments $22.5m, current assets $344.3m vs current liabilities $39.6m (ratio ≈8.7). Working capital is $304.7m; interest income ($8.0m) exceeds interest expense ($44k). The ratio is driven by a large liquid asset base (cash, investments, and taxes receivable $46.9m) against thin trade/current obligations — not a Yahoo mapping error. Residual caveat: $162m Pinago Utama deal (alternate news, marketscreener.com, 5 May 2025) is not reflected in FY2025 Yahoo M&A cash flow ($0.001m “Purchase Of Business”), so post-deal funding may sit off this snapshot.

SourcesTried: filings_bodies, filings_index, yahoo_financials, news_manifest, alternate_news, screening_snapshot

NextSources: none for the D/E and current-ratio mechanics; Company IR / FY25 results presentation for Pinago funding and post-acquisition debt if any

Timing neutral (RSI 51). Verdict: accumulate — deepest quantitative support and clean balance sheet, with neutral entry timing.

Yahoo FY2025 supports the value inputs (net income $90.9m, FCF $83.6m, OCF $113.8m, ROE ~15.7% on reported equity). Gap-fill undermines the **Consumer Defensive** sector tag: revenue volatility and Indonesia policy sensitivity (news/filings headlines) are commodity/agricultural, not staple-defensive. Lynch PEG, Piotroski, and Earnings Quality outcomes are not in local JSON; Yahoo OCF exists, so the Earnings Quality fail may reflect a screen ingest gap rather than absent cash generation. FCF yield 12.1% is cited in the red-flag pass but not stored in `screening_snapshot.json`.

SourcesTried: filings_bodies, filings_index, yahoo_financials, news_manifest, alternate_news, screening_snapshot
